region_of_origin: Global marine waters (Atlantic, Pacific, Indian Ocean)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw lobster is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium lobster tail or a generous portion of lobster meat before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Crustaceans, lobster, northern, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15147
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw lobster (cr_id F03050015, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. These values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 15147 (Crustaceans, lobster, northern, raw), which is cited here as the named approved source for template compliance and for supplementary micronutrient and fatty acid sub-fraction values not reported by HPB. All values cross-validated between both approved sources; no discrepancies exceed expected inter-species variation.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw lobster contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by calculation. No GI database entry exists or is required for this food type.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 423 mg per 100 g — borderline high; potassium 200 mg per 100 g provides partial offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit raw lobster to moderate portions (≤150 g) prepared without added salt, butter, or soy-based sauces, as the naturally elevated sodium of 423 mg/100 g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium intake; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables helps partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein 16.52 g per 100 g supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw lobster is an excellent protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes, containing 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, which produces no glycaemic response; high protein content slows gastric emptying and supports post-meal glucose control, provided it is prepared without high-carbohydrate or high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.14 g/100 g) and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but cholesterol is elevated at 127 mg/100 g and sodium is 423 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw lobster offers a lean protein profile with low saturated fat and omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health, but the cholesterol content of 127 mg/100 g and sodium of 423 mg/100 g warrant moderation in individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia; steaming or grilling without added butter or salt is the preferred preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), selenium 36 mcg/100 g, and low saturated fat support anti-inflammatory profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw lobster provides omega-3 fatty acids, selenium, and minimal saturated fat, which collectively support an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; however, the high sodium content means preparation without added salt is essential to avoid exacerbating tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw lobster (Lobster, raw) is a premium marine crustacean consumed worldwide, characterised by a dark blue-black shell that turns bright red upon cooking, white to translucent flesh, and a firm, succulent texture with a naturally sweet, briny flavour. It belongs to the food group Fish and seafood, subgroup Crustaceans, and is classified as a raw, minimally processed whole food. Per 100 g, raw lobster provides 77 kcal, 16.52 g protein, 0.75 g fat (of which 0.14 g is saturated), 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 0 g dietary fibre, 423 mg sodium, 200 mg potassium, 84 mg calcium, 0.26 mg iron, and 127 mg cholesterol. It is an excellent source of lean protein, selenium (36 mcg/100 g), zinc (2.79 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (1.3 mcg/100 g), and phosphorus (185 mg/100 g). The negligible carbohydrate content means glycemic index is not applicable. Clinically, raw lobster is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its zero carbohydrate and high protein content. Its sodium level of 423 mg/100 g places it in the borderline range for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, requiring careful preparation without added salt or butter. The omega-3 fatty acid content and low saturated fat profile are beneficial for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory purposes. Raw lobster is a major crustacean shellfish allergen and is not kosher. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Common preparations include steaming, grilling, boiling, and serving as sashimi-style raw preparations in certain cuisines.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in lobster?
Is lobster good for heart health?
How many calories in raw lobster?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03050015, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source, with full cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 15147 (Crustaceans, lobster, northern, raw). All core macros and secondary nutrients are consistent between both approved sources. Fatty acid sub-fractions and micronutrients supplemented from USDA SR Legacy where HPB did not report them, with values consistent with the lobster species profile.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero: raw lobster is a pure animal protein with no carbohydrate content, confirmed by both HPB Foodo (cr_id F03050015) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 15147. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as lobster is an animal food containing no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy entry 15147). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Vitamin_c_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA SR Legacy reports negligible vitamin C for raw lobster but specific values vary by species and are not reliably reported for this entry. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo; USDA SR Legacy reports negligible vitamin A for raw lobster. Folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo; USDA SR Legacy reports small amounts below reliable detection thresholds for this entry. Fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated_fat_g 0.14, monounsaturated_fat_g 0.13, polyunsaturated_fat_g 0.22) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 15147 as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid breakdown; values are consistent with the lobster lipid profile. Micronutrients vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water_g supplemented from USDA SR Legacy entry 15147 where not reported by HPB. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03411433 and FoodEx2 code A00LN are defensible best-match codes for lobster as a food item; SNOMED CT code 226888001 is the standard food substance code for lobster. Halal status: lobster is considered halal under the majority Shafi'i school position followed in Singapore and Malaysia; kosher status is false as shellfish are not kosher. The rheumatoid arthritis health context note in the input incorrectly stated 'No positive anti-inflammatory factor present' while simultaneously noting omega-3 fatty acids and selenium — this contradiction has been corrected in the output to accurately reflect the anti-inflammatory nutritional profile. Multilingual local_names are null as lobster is a generic international ingredient without a single dominant cuisine origin requiring mandatory local-language names under the Veritas multilingual rules.
